text: Here is the angiography of the patient's left corner artery showing a 70% stenosis of the left anterior descending artery and a second video of the 90% stenosis of the main right corner artery.
Preparation of the operative field for left internal thoracic artery harvest.
The head of the patient is on the left.
After clearing the pleura with cautery and finger dissection without entering the thoracic cavity if possible, the chest wall is exposed one half centimeter beyond the lateral vein.
Intact pleura prevents individual lung lobes from popping into the operative field at inopportune moments.
The safest start point
is the caudal end near the xiphy sternum, but the artery may not always be visible here, hidden by muscle or fascia.

It is usually seen one-third of the distance up from the caudal end, and if one draws an imaginary line from the visible artery to a point at the end of the sternum, incising over this imaginary line will expose it most of the time.
Inadvertent damage at this level is usually inconsequential.

The first step is incision of the fascia with Metzenbaum's scissors in the crevice between the medial thoracic vein and the artery itself, so that there will be no overhang of tissue precluding complete view of the artery.
One does not want to be operating under a cliff.
The author prefers the control of scissors over the harmonic scalpel because of the variable thickness of the patient's pleura and textural feedback.
After the preparation phase, the harvest stage with the harmonic scalpel can be divided into three.
One, the startup at the caudal end, two, the middle segment, and three, the finish at the cephalid end.
Cautery is used to stop chest wall bleeding, but clips or fine sutures are used for bleeding internal thoracic artery branches.
Before use, the harmonic hook blade should be positioned for ideal length and orientation, going from right to left, as in harvest of the left mammary artery.
The hook faces opposite to the direction of harvest.
The hook is very sharp, and inadvertent movements can cut branches and even the mammary before intended.
It can also be extended to reach ITAs in patients with wide sterna.
The mammary artery is first bared over a small area without power to identify its borders.
One can hold the mammary by the adventitia with delicate forceps, such as ringed forceps, without fear of damage.
As one moves along the ITA, it is prudent to free the artery in 360 degrees so that proximal and distal control is possible
if suture repair of an avulsed branch is needed.
Branches are first isolated on either side to expose their exact width, then using forcep prongs on either side of the branch, depressing the artery so as to lengthen the branch.

The double prong technique, the blunt end of the hook is used to sit on the branch, at least one millimeter away from the artery, but not touching the mammary veins until the branch turns white or black.
and breaks naturally with gentle pressure.
One quickly learns the tissues of a patient.
Sometimes a very slight traction on a branch will result in tearing or an adventitial hematoma.
But if one is still at the distal end of the mammary, no harm is done.
The hook can be used to finally cut the branch, but it must be completely sealed.
This takes a little trial and error to learn how to hook slowly.
With the harmonic hook blade on a low power of two of a possible five, one paints the artery in a sweeping motion with the non-hook side of the harmonic scalpel.
The artery can be actually touched briefly with this painting technique without fear of thermal damage.
As long as one does not dwell on the artery, tissues adherent to the artery will part like the Red Sea and expose the branches.
A frequently asked question is, do the harmonically sealed branches ever start bleeding?
If a branch is to bleed, it will have done so usually before the end of the harvest.
In 14 and a half years, the author has yet to take a patient back for bleeding from an improperly sealed branch.
One real advantage of harmonic technology is the lack of clips, especially useful in sequential graphs.
This author averages one clip per internal thoracic artery.
Additionally, when the mammary artery dilates, when spasm from the handling is relieved, there are no indents seen at the clip locations, which might reduce turbulence of flow over the years.
If the crevice incised was not quite in the right place, one can use the hook to remove this overhang of tissue to better expose the ITA and its branches.
Damage to an artery with the harmonic scalpel rarely occurs.

In this author's experience with the traditional skeletonization technique, an average of 1 in 20 arteries were damaged, whereas 9 of 2,388 mammary arteries, or 0.37%, were damaged beyond the point of any use.
Possibly the dividing of tissues by the harmonic rather than the dragging of tissues by the cautery tip contributes to decreased injury, especially in fragile arteries.
There can be difficult dissections with the harmonic.
In particular, patients with a bleeding tendency due to plavix or heparin on board or with elderly fragile tissues, harvest can be tedious and frustrating.
When bleeding occurs from a branch, the harmonic cannot coagulate as with cautery.
necessitating the use of a clip, except sometimes if the patient is an active smoker.
If bleeding is only slight, maintaining a little pressure while continuing to dissect the ITA often results in cessation of bleeding.
When first encountering a branch, the edges of the branch are first isolated without power
until one can see at least two-thirds deep on either side.
Then power is used to free the ITA circumferentially.
Because the hook end of the harmonic scalpel is only two millimeters wide, one need only sit on the branch about one millimeter away from the ITA until the branch gives away.
One should not move up and down on the branch.
There is no need, and doing so may injure the IT veins and cause bleeding.
Skeletonization with the harmonic scalpel is in contrast to skeletonization with the cautery tip as a dissector.
In this latter technique, all the medial branches can be isolated, then clipped and cut, followed by all the lateral branches.
However, with the harmonic scalpel, circumferential harvesting is quicker and safer.
One simple trick for ITA exposure is to use surgical gauze to press, not rub, the ITA with your thumb.
This tip displaces any obscuring soft fat and a clean ITA will appear.
Note also that branches are not cauterized but sealed.
This is achieved by the blade tip vibrating in the range of 55,500 Hz.
At the top end, one follows the curve of the internal thoracic artery with the harmonic scalpel, always maintaining the hook blade in the same perpendicular orientation to the ITA.
God gave us five fingers, and they are all pushed to good use for exposure at the top end.
The thumb and fifth finger are used for bracing.
The ring finger depresses the pleura to expose the ITA, and the index and second finger distract the ITA in the caudal direction to expose and lengthen branches for sealing.
The harmonic can seal branches up to five millimeters in diameter, and actually,
Harmonic sealing is preferred because clips fall often, more often than sealed branches bleed.
For the right internal thoracic artery, right-handed surgeons start the pleural incision between the ITA and medial IT vein at the cephalid end.
However, the medial vein of the RITA can be quite a bit lower than on the left side, so inadvertent damage to the ITA is more likely, no matter
It can be used as a free or Y graft.
After the pleural incision is completed, dissection of the RITA starts at the caudal end as for the LITA dissection.
The point of initial ITA dissection is the most likely place for injury.
Hence, commencing the dissection in the midpoint risks having two halves of an internal thoracic artery instead of one full length segment.
by sealing a branch at least one millimeter away from the ITA.
If it does bleed, there is a stump to clip.
The ITA can dilate without indenting, and there is less chance of ITA damage.
Going further than one millimeter risks causing bleeding from the internal thoracic veins.
The technique of skeletonization with a little practice becomes quicker than the cautery tip method, usually taking 15 to 18 minutes per mammary.
If one has never skeletonized before, the learning curve reaches 80% comfort level after approximately 20 internal thoracic artery dissections.
If the technique of skeletonization is already known, likely 10 dissections will afford the same level.
When teaching residents, staged instruction of the different segments works the best.
They start at the easiest stage, the middle segment, and are allowed 20 minutes by the clock.
They progress to the proximal part, then the start up, then the incising in the crevice.
This method of teaching is analogous to memorizing a piano piece by starting at the end, from the last two bars, then the last four bars, et cetera.
By the time residents are familiar with the whole procedure, they have become most familiar with the IMA harmonic scalpel segment.
Any smoke that you see during the harvest is not smoke, but steam.
It does not carry particles that can be inhaled, as when cautery is used.
Sometimes there may be two branches quite close together, as shown in this clip.
At the top end, in order to seal the final branch, the first intercostal, one can operate through a window, a triangular space created by the sternum, the medial IT vein, and cephalid soft tissue.
Preserving the medial
IT vein is not vital to the patient, but helps prevent bleeding at sternal closure.
Because blood flows in the caudal to cephalid direction, occlusion at the top end results in a sausage-like vein, very tempting for wires to pierce.
To stop bleeding, IT veins can never be just cauterized.
They must be clipped or sewn.

The five-finger position for the top end of the rita harvest is similar to the left, with the fourth finger holding the pleura-covered lung away and the first and second distracting the ITA caudally to expose the branches.
Once the first intercostal is sealed, there is often a more medial branch heading south and medially from the ITA.
Circumferentially freeing the ITA will help discover this branch.
Once these last two branches are sealed,
one can pass the index finger upward along the ITA, and if no obstruction is felt, this verifies that the harvest is completed.
For the Rita, operating through the window is required more often than for the left.
To summarize, the four stages are 1.
The preparation with sweeping the pleura lateral to the internal thoracic artery and IT veins and incising the parietal pleura to expose the length of the artery.
The start of the ITA dissection at the caudal end.
The middle segment harvest with the sealing of multiple branches.
The top end with sealing of the first intercostal and medial branch for maximum length.

To finally verify the anastomosis, epicardial ultrasound is used in two planes to ensure graft patency, seen here for the LEMA to the LAD in the longitudinal view and the cross-sectional view, and then two views of the right internal thoracic artery to the posterior descending artery, the longitudinal view and the cross-sectional view.
